text: The surgeon retracts the sigmoid colon out of the pelvis, exposing the anterior aspect of the promontory on the right side of the sigmoid mesentery using scissors with monopolar cautery. The old mesh is visible, fixed to the right part of the promontory and the lateral part of the middle rectum, illustrating the recurrence of rectal prolapse.

The surgeon retracts the sigmoid colon out of the pelvis, exposing the anterior aspect of the promontory on the right side of the sigmoid mesentery using scissors with monopolar cautery. The old mesh is visibly fixed to the right part of the promontory and the lateral part of the middle rectum, illustrating the recurrence of the rectal prolapse.

The surgeon dissects the peritoneum over the Douglas pouch, revealing the levator ani muscle. The position of the initial mesh is visually compared to the operator's finger during a digital rectal exam.

The surgeon dissects the peritoneum over the Douglas pouch, revealing the levator ani muscle. The position of the initial mesh is visually compared to the operator's finger during a digital rectal exam, highlighting its high placement.
The surgeon introduces a bioprosthetic collagen porcine mesh and secures it to the anterior aspect of the lower rectum using five non-absorbable polyethylene-coated braided polyester stitches.
A bioprosthetic collagen porcine mesh is introduced and fixed onto the anterior aspect of the lower rectum using five non-absorbable polyethylene-coated braided polyester stitches.

The surgeon fastens the new mesh to the old mesh using two non-absorbable sutures. The promontory is dissected, leaving the old mesh in place. The anterior sacroiliac ligament at the sacral promontory is exposed, and a non-absorbable stitch is used to secure the mesh to the sacrum.

The surgeon fastens the new mesh to the old mesh with two non-absorbable sutures. The promontory is dissected, leaving the old mesh in place. The anterior sacroiliac ligament at the sacral promontory is exposed, and a non-absorbable stitch is used to secure the mesh to the sacrum.

The surgeon closes the Douglas pouch, creating a passage for the sigmoid colon. The peritoneum is then closed over the meshes using two continuous barbed absorbable sutures, effectively isolating the meshes from the abdominal cavity.
